questions.text: Which of the following compound is oxidized to get methyl ethyl ketone:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 2-butanol (also known as sec-butanol) undergoes oxidation to form methyl ethyl ketone (MEK). The oxidation typically involves the removal of hydrogen from the alcohol group of 2-butanol, resulting in the formation of a ketone group in methyl ethyl ketone. The balanced chemical equation for the oxidation of 2-butanol to methyl ethyl ketone can be represented as follows: 2-butanol (rightarrow) Methyl Ethyl Ketone +Water This reaction is often carried out using oxidizing agents, such as chromates or dichromates. The process converts the secondary alcohol group in 2-butanol to a ketone functional group in methyl ethyl ketone through the loss of hydrogen atoms. Therefore this option is not correct.

2-butanol (also known as sec-butanol) undergoes oxidation to form methyl ethyl ketone (MEK). The oxidation typically involves the removal of hydrogen from the alcohol group of 2-butanol, resulting in the formation of a ketone group in methyl ethyl ketone. The process converts the secondary alcohol group in 2-butanol to a ketone functional group in methyl ethyl ketone through the loss of hydrogen atoms. Therefore this option is not correct.

The process converts the secondary alcohol group in 2-butanol to a ketone functional group in methyl ethyl ketone through the loss of hydrogen atoms. Therefore this option is correct.

questions.text: Catalytic reduction of acetone will produce
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Methyl alcohol (methanol): The catalytic reduction of acetone does not produce methanol. Methanol is a simpler alcohol and is not formed from the reduction of a ketone like acetone.

Isopropyl alcohol (2-propanol): This is the correct product of the catalytic reduction of acetone. The carbonyl group in acetone is reduced to a hydroxyl group, leading to the formation of isopropyl alcohol.

Ethyl alcohol (ethanol): The catalytic reduction of acetone does not yield ethanol. Ethanol is a primary alcohol, and the reduction of a ketone like acetone results in the formation of a secondary alcohol, specifically isopropyl alcohol.

Neopentyl alcohol: Neopentyl alcohol refers to 2,2-dimethyl-1-propanol. The catalytic reduction of acetone does not directly produce neopentyl alcohol. Neopentyl alcohol has a more complex structure and is not a typical product of the reduction of a simple ketone like acetone.

questions.explanation: Acetone is a 3—C ketone. On reduction, it will produce a 3—C secondary alcohol.

questions.text: Which of the following will not give addition reaction with NaHSO3
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Formaldehyde will undergo addition reaction with NaHSO3. The bisulfite ion (HSO3-) adds to the carbonyl group, forming a bisulfite addition product.
Acetaldehyde will also undergo addition reaction with NaHSO3. Similar to formaldehyde, the bisulfite ion adds to the carbonyl group, forming a bisulfite addition product.
Propionaldehyde will undergo addition reaction with NaHSO3. The bisulfite ion adds to the carbonyl group, resulting in a bisulfite addition product.

Ethanol is not a carbonyl compound; it is an alcohol. Therefore, it will not undergo addition reaction with NaHSO3. The bisulfite addition reaction typically involves carbonyl compounds, and alcohols do not possess a carbonyl group.

questions.explanation: Aldehydes and small methyl ketones react with a saturated aqueous solution of sodium bisulphite to form a crystalline white precipitate of sodium bisulphite adduct. Alcohols do not react with NaHSO3.

questions.text: Which one of the following organic compounds does not give iodoform test?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Ethanal (acetaldehyde) gives the iodoform test. The iodoform test is a chemical test used to detect the presence of methyl ketones. Ethanal is a methyl ketone, so it will give a positive iodoform test. The iodoform test works on the principle that methyl ketones can react with iodine and sodium hydroxide to produce a yellow precipitate of iodoform (CHI3). The reaction is as follows:CH3COCH3 + 3I2 + 4NaOH → CHI3 + 3NaI + 3NaHCO3 + H2O

Secondary alcohols give the iodoform test. The iodoform test is a chemical test used to detect the presence of methyl ketones. It is a qualitative test, which means that it can only detect the presence or absence of a methyl ketone. It cannot be used to quantify the amount of a methyl ketone in a sample

Yes, methyl ketones give the iodoform test. The iodoform test is a chemical test used to detect the presence of methyl ketones. It is a qualitative test, which means that it can only detect the presence or absence of methyl ketones in a sample. It cannot be used to quantify the amount of a methyl ketone in a sample. The iodoform test works on the principle that methyl ketones can be oxidized by iodine in the presence of base to form iodoform (CHI3), a yellow precipitate. The reaction is as follows: R-CO-CH3 + 3I2 + 4NaOH → CHI3 + R-COO-Na+ + 3NaI + 3H2O

Methanol (CH3OH) does not give iodoform test. The iodoform test is a chemical test used to detect the presence of methyl ketones. It is a qualitative test, which means that it can only detect the presence or absence of a methyl ketone. It cannot be used to quantify the amount of a methyl ketone in a sample.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct.Methanol (CH3OH) does not give iodoform test. The iodoform test is a chemical test used to detect the presence of methyl ketones. It is a qualitative test, which means that it can only detect the presence or absence of a methyl ketone. It cannot be used to quantify the amount of a methyl ketone in a sample.

questions.text: Which of the following is incorrect statement?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Yes, 1o alcohols on oxidation give aldehydes. The reaction is called oxidation reaction. In an oxidation reaction, an oxidizing agent removes electrons from a molecule. In this case, the oxidizing agent is potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and the electrons are removed from the hydroxyl group of the 1o alcohol, forming an aldehyde. The reaction is shown below: RCH2OH + K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 → RCHO + K2SO4 + H2O

2o alcohols on oxidation give ketones. The reaction is called oxidation reaction. In an oxidation reaction, an oxidizing agent removes electrons from a molecule. In this case, the oxidizing agent is potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and the electrons are removed from the hydroxyl group of the 2o alcohol, forming a ketone. The reaction is shown below: R2CHOH + K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 → R2C=O + K2SO4 + H2O

options.text: On dry distillation of calcium methanoate, ethanol is produced
options.explanation: No, on dry distillation of calcium methanoate, acetaldehyde is produced. The reaction is called dry distillation. In dry distillation, a compound is heated in the absence of air. This causes the compound to decompose into simpler molecules. The reaction of calcium methanoate is shown below: Ca(HCOO)2 → CH3CHO + CO2 Here, Ca(HCOO)2 represents calcium methanoate.

options.text: On dry distillation of calcium ethanoate, propanone is produced
options.explanation: Yes, on dry distillation of calcium ethanoate, propanone is produced. The reaction is called Dry distillation. In dry distillation, a compound is heated in the absence of air. This causes the compound to decompose into simpler molecules. The reaction of calcium ethanoate is shown below: Ca(C2H3O2)2 → CH3COCH3 + CO2 Here, Ca(C2H3O2)2 represents calcium ethanoate.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct.No, on dry distillation of calcium methanoate, acetaldehyde is produced. The reaction is called dry distillation. In dry distillation, a compound is heated in the absence of air. This causes the compound to decompose into simpler molecules. The reaction of calcium methanoate is shown below: Ca(HCOO)2 → CH3CHO + CO2 Here, Ca(HCOO)2 represents calcium methanoate.

options.explanation: Aldehydes and ketones possessing a-hydrogen atoms react with acold dilute solution of an alkali to form addition products known asaldols. Aldehydes that have no a -hydrogen atoms undergo Cannizzaro’s reaction.

questions.text: Which one of the following can undergo aldol condensation reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: It cannot undergo aldol condensation reaction. This is due to the necessary presence of alpha hydrogen.
Benzaldehyde does not have alpha hydrogen hence it does not undergo aldol condensation instead it undergoes cannizaros reaction.
Acetaldehyde has alpha hydrogen that is the hydrogen attached to the carbon adjacent to carbonyl carbon hence it undergoes cannizaros reaction.
Trimethylacetaldehyde has no alpha hydrogen hence it does not undergo aldol condensation.

questions.explanation: Acetaldehyde has alpha hydrogen that is the hydrogen attached to the carbon adjacent to carbonyl carbon hence it undergoes cannizaros reaction.

questions.text: Reaction of aldehydes with Tollen's reagent results in formation of _?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Option A is not the correct result of the reaction between aldehydes and Tollen's reagent. A red precipitate is not formed when aldehydes react with Tollen's reagent.
Option B is not the correct result of the reaction between aldehydes and Tollen's reagent. A yellow precipitate is not formed when aldehydes react with Tollen's reagent.

Option C is the correct result of the reaction between aldehydes and Tollen's reagent. When aldehydes react with Tollens' reagent (ammoniacal silver nitrate), they are oxidized to carboxylic acids, and silver ions (Ag+) in Tollens' reagent are reduced to metallic silver (Ag). This reduction of silver ions results in the formation of a silver mirror on the inner surface of the reaction vessel.
Option D is not the correct result of the reaction between aldehydes and Tollen's reagent. A brick red precipitate is not formed when aldehydes react with Tollen's reagent.

questions.explanation: The correct result of the reaction between aldehydes and Tollen's reagent is C) Silver mirror. The formation of a silver mirror is a characteristic test used to detect the presence of an aldehyde functional group in the compound being tested.

questions.text: Which of the following compounds is present in camphor and menthone?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Neither camphor nor menthone contains an aldehyde functional group. Aldehydes have a formyl group (-CHO) at the end of a carbon chain, and there is no such group in camphor or menthone.
Neither camphor nor menthone contains an alcohol functional group. Alcohols have a hydroxyl group (-OH) bonded to a carbon atom, and there is no such group in camphor or menthone.

Neither camphor nor menthone is an ester. Esters have a carbonyl group (C=O) and an oxygen atom bonded to an alkyl or aryl group. Camphor and Menthone do not have this combination of functional groups.

Both camphor and menthone are ketones. Ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) in the middle of a carbon chain. In camphor, the carbonyl group is part of a fused ring system, and in menthone, it is part of an open-chain structure.

questions.explanation: Both camphor and menthone are ketones. Ketones have a carbonyl group (C=O) in the middle of a carbon chain. In camphor, the carbonyl group is part of a fused ring system, and in menthone, it is part of an open-chain structure. Therefore, option d is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following products is formed in the Cannizzaro's reaction?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Aldol: An aldol is a type of organic compound that is formed by the condensation of two aldehydes or ketones. The Cannizzaro's reaction does not produce aldols.
The Cannizzaro reaction is a redox reaction that produces a carboxylic salt and an alcohol. The reaction is named after Stanislao Cannizzaro, who first described it in 1853.
The Cannizzaro reaction is a redox reaction that produces a carboxylic salt and an alcohol. The reaction is named after Stanislao Cannizzaro, who first described it in 1853.

Unsaturated reaction: The Cannizzaro reaction is not an unsaturated reaction. Unsaturated reactions are reactions that involve the addition of a double bond to a molecule. The Cannizzaro reaction does not involve the addition of a double bond to a molecule.

questions.explanation: The Cannizzaro reaction is a redox reaction that produces a carboxylic salt and an alcohol. The reaction is named after Stanislao Cannizzaro, who first described it in 1853. Therefore, option b is correct.

questions.text: Fehling's solution is a complex of:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Fehling's solution is a complex of cupric tartrate.
This option is incorrect. Fehling's solution is a complex of cupric tartrate.
This option is incorrect. Fehling's solution is a complex of cupric tartrate.

Fehling's solution is a complex of cupric tartrate. As mentioned earlier, Fehling's solution B contains sodium potassium tartrate (a chelating agent that complexes with cupric ions) in a basic solution. The complexation of cupric ions with tartrate ions helps prevent the precipitation of cuprous oxide in the absence of an aldehyde.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is d) Cupric tartrate. Fehling solution is a complex of cupric tartrate.

questions.text: Propanone does not undergo:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Propanone (also known as acetone) can undergo oxime formation. Oxime formation involves the reaction of a ketone or aldehyde with hydroxylamine to produce an oxime. The reaction typically occurs at the carbonyl group.
Propanone can undergo hydrazone formation with hydrazine. The reaction involves the addition of hydrazine to the carbonyl group of propanone, forming a hydrazone.

Propanone does not undergo reduction with Fehling solution. Fehling's solution is commonly used to test for the presence of aldehydes, which can be oxidized to carboxylic acids. Since propanone is a ketone and not an aldehyde, it does not react with Fehling solution.
Propanone can undergo a reaction with hydrogen cyanide (HCN) to form a cyanohydrin. This reaction involves the addition of HCN to the carbonyl group of propanone.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is c) Reduction with Fehling solution. Propanone does not undergo reduction with Fehling solution because it is a ketone and not an aldehyde.

questions.text: Consider the following reaction:R—CHO + 2[Ag(NH3)2]OH + R—COONH4 + 2Ag + 2NH3 + H2OThis reaction represents:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The Fehling test is another chemical test used to detect the presence of reducing sugars, just like the Benedict's test. It's named after its developer, Hermann von Fehling. In this test, a sample is mixed with Fehling's solution, which is a combination of Fehling's A (copper sulfate) and Fehling's B (sodium potassium tartrate in sodium hydroxide). The mixture is then heated. If a reducing sugar is present, it will react with the copper ions in Fehling's solution, resulting in the formation of a reddish-brown precipitate of copper(I) oxide. This color change indicates the presence of a reducing sugar. The Fehling test is commonly used in the laboratory to identify reducing sugars in various substances.

False!The Benedict's test is another chemical test used to detect the presence of reducing sugars, including certain aldehydes and ketones. In this test, a sample is mixed with Benedict's reagent, which is a solution of copper sulfate (CuSO4) and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3). The mixture is then heated. If a reducing sugar is present, it will react with the copper ions in the Benedict's reagent, resulting in the formation of a colored precipitate. The color change can range from green to yellow, orange, or even brick red, depending on the amount of reducing sugar present.

False!The reaction you mentioned is actually the Tollens test. It's a chemical test that helps us detect the presence of aldehydes. In this test, an aldehyde (represented by R-CHO) reacts with Tollens' reagent, which is a mixture of silver nitrate (AgNO3) and ammonia (NH3). The reaction produces a silver mirror on the inner surface of the reaction vessel. This silver mirror formation is a positive result for the presence of an aldehyde.

The reaction you mentioned is actually the Tollens test. It's a chemical test that helps us detect the presence of aldehydes. In this test, an aldehyde (represented by R-CHO) reacts with Tollens' reagent, which is a mixture of silver nitrate (AgNO3) and ammonia (NH3). The reaction produces a silver mirror on the inner surface of the reaction vessel. This silver mirror formation is a positive result for the presence of an aldehyde.

questions.explanation: The reaction you mentioned is actually the Tollens test. It's a chemical test that helps us detect the presence of aldehydes. In this test, an aldehyde (represented by R-CHO) reacts with Tollens' reagent, which is a mixture of silver nitrate (AgNO3) and ammonia (NH3). The reaction produces a silver mirror on the inner surface of the reaction vessel. This silver mirror formation is a positive result for the presence of an aldehyde.

questions.text: Which reagent is responsible for the conversion of ketone to secondary alcohol:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: False!When it comes to converting a ketone to a secondary alcohol, sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is the reagent you're looking for. NaBH4 is a powerful reducing agent that can add a hydride ion (H-) to the carbonyl group of a ketone. This reduction reaction results in the formation of a secondary alcohol. NaBH4 is commonly used in organic chemistry labs for this purpose because it is selective and relatively mild in its reaction conditions.

When it comes to converting a ketone to a secondary alcohol, sodium borohydride (NaBH4) is the reagent you're looking for. NaBH4 is a powerful reducing agent that can add a hydride ion (H-) to the carbonyl group of a ketone. This reduction reaction results in the formation of a secondary alcohol. NaBH4 is commonly used in organic chemistry labs for this purpose because it is selective and relatively mild in its reaction conditions.

questions.text: An alcohol is converted to an aldehyde with same number of carbon aroms as that of alcohol in the presence of K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 alcohol is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: False!When CH3CH2CH2OH, which is 1-propanol, is treated with K2Cr2O7/H2SO4, it undergoes oxidation and gets converted into an aldehyde. The resulting aldehyde will have the same number of carbon atoms as the original alcohol, so in this case, it would be propanal (CH3CH2CHO). The K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 acts as an oxidizing agent, helping in the conversion of the alcohol group to the aldehyde group.

The K2Cr2O7/H2SO4 acts as an oxidizing agent, helping in the conversion of the alcohol group to the aldehyde group.
